walkthrough.title: Remove 5-Player Cards
walkthrough.text: Before we set anything up, grab the card box and look for any cards with a small 5-player icon on them. If you are playing with 2 to 4 players, pull those cards out and set them aside in the box. They are only used for the 5-player variant. With that done, let's build the play area.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Monster Cards
walkthrough.text: Find all 48 Monster cards. These have a dark, menacing look with a creature illustration on the front. Sort them into stacks by the Area icon in the upper right corner of each card. There are eight possible areas: Hills, Ruins, Forest, Swamp, Mountains, Caverns, Barrens, and Valley. You will only use 5 of these stacks in a single game. For your first game, we recommend Hills, Ruins, Forest, Valley, and Mountains.

walkthrough.title: Arrange Monster Stacks
walkthrough.text: Within each Monster stack, arrange the cards face-up so the weakest Monster (lowest Strength) is on top, the strongest is on the bottom, and the middle one is in between. Then place the 5 stacks in a row to form the top row of the Center Stacks, ordered from lowest Strength on the left to highest on the right. For the recommended first game, left to right: Hills (Goblin on top), Ruins (Skeleton on top), Forest (Treant on top), Valley (Owlbear on top), Mountains (Dire Bear on top). Return any unused Monster stacks to the box.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Citizen Cards
walkthrough.text: Now find the 108 Citizen cards. Sort them into stacks by their name, so all Monks together, all Clerics together, and so on. Each Citizen type has an Activation Number in the upper left corner. You will choose one Citizen type per Activation Number. For your first game, use: Cleric (1), Merchant (2), Mercenary (3), Archer (4), Peasant (5) in one row, and Knight (6), Rogue (7), Champion (8), Paladin (9), Butcher (10) in the next row.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Citizen Stacks
walkthrough.text: For each chosen Citizen type, remove 1 card from the stack and place it back in the box. You should have exactly 5 cards per stack. Place the stacks face-up in sequential order by Activation Number to form the two middle rows of the Center Stacks, five stacks per row. The grid should now have four rows and five columns: Monsters on top, two rows of Citizens in the middle, and an empty bottom row for Domains. Return any unchosen Citizen stacks to the box.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Domain Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 24 Domain cards together. Deal 2 face-down cards to each of the 5 positions in the bottom row, then deal 1 face-up card on top of each position. That gives you 3 cards per stack with only the top card visible. Return any unused Domains to the box. Domains are buildings and territories you can acquire to expand your kingdom.

walkthrough.title: Place Exhausted Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the Exhausted cards. These are the cards that go on empty stacks when a stack runs out during the game. Take 2 Exhausted cards per player and place them in a stack above the Monster row. So for a 3-player game, you need 6 Exhausted cards. Return the rest to the box.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starter and Reference Cards
walkthrough.text: Give each player 1 Starter Peasant card, 1 Starter Knight card, and 1 Reference card. The Starter cards have a special back and are different from the regular Citizens. They form the beginning of each player's tableau. Important: Starter Peasant and Starter Knight do not have Role icons, so they do not count toward Domain requirements. Return any unused Starter and Reference cards to the box.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Give each player 2 Gold tokens and 1 Magic token. Place the remaining tokens within easy reach of everyone to form the Bank. There are 50 Gold, 50 Strength, 50 Magic, and 25 Victory Point tokens in the game. Resources and VP tokens are considered unlimited. If tokens run out, use the 12 Multiplier tokens included in the box. For example, 1 Gold on a x5 multiplier represents 5 Gold.

walkthrough.title: Deal Duke Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all 10 Duke cards and deal 2 face-down to each player. Each player secretly looks at their 2 Dukes, chooses 1 to keep for the game, and returns the other to the box without showing anyone. Your Duke is kept secret until the end of the game. The Duke determines how you earn bonus Victory Points during scoring, so take a moment to read your Duke's scoring condition. It will shape your strategy.

walkthrough.title: Select the First Player
walkthrough.text: Randomly select one player to be the first Active Player. Give them the First Player token and the 2 six-sided dice. Play will proceed clockwise from the first player. You are now ready to play Valeria: Card Kingdoms!

First game row 1: Cleric, Merchant, Mercenary, Archer, Peasant
First game row 2: Knight, Rogue, Champion, Paladin, Butcher

Place in a stack above the Monster row
Return extras to box

summary.title: Select the First Player
bullets: Randomly choose the first Active Player
Give them the First Player token and 2 dice
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview: Four Phases
walkthrough.text: Every turn in Valeria: Card Kingdoms follows four phases in order. Phase 1 is the Roll Phase, where the Active Player rolls the dice. Phase 2 is the Harvest Phase, where all players collect resources from their Citizens. Phase 3 is the Action Phase, where the Active Player takes 2 actions. Phase 4 is the End Phase, where the dice pass to the left and the game checks for end conditions. Let's walk through each phase in detail.

walkthrough.title: Roll Phase
walkthrough.text: The Active Player rolls both dice. Each individual die value and the sum of both dice will activate Citizens in the Harvest Phase. So if you roll a 3 and a 5, you activate Citizens with Activation Numbers 3, 5, and 8. Before moving on, the Active Player may use any Domain cards they own that manipulate dice values. Each die-changing Domain can only be used once per Roll Phase. Once the Active Player is satisfied, they announce the final dice values and play moves to the Harvest Phase.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Phase: How Citizens Activate
walkthrough.text: All players participate in the Harvest Phase, not just the Active Player. Each Citizen card has two powers at the bottom. The left-side power (under the sword icon) is the on-turn power and only applies to the Active Player. The right-side power (under the shield icon) is the off-turn power and only applies to non-Active Players. When a Citizen's Activation Number matches one of the dice results, that Citizen activates and the matching power triggers.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Phase: Multiple Copies and Doubles
walkthrough.text: If you own multiple copies of a Citizen, each one activates individually. So two Archers with Activation Number 4 each trigger separately when a 4 is rolled. Rolling doubles is even better: each matching card activates twice. If you have three Archers and roll double 4s, each Archer fires for the first 4 and again for the second 4, giving you six total activations. The Thief is special: when activated, the Thief always acts first, before any other Citizens harvest. Resources come from the Bank unless a card says otherwise.

walkthrough.title: Harvest Phase: No Activations
walkthrough.text: If the dice roll does not activate any of your Citizens at all, you get a consolation prize. You may take any 1 resource of your choice (Gold, Strength, or Magic) from the Bank. This applies whether you are the Active Player or not. You can also activate your Citizens in any order you wish during the Harvest Phase, and you can immediately spend resources gained from one Citizen to fuel another Citizen's power.

walkthrough.title: Action Phase: Your Two Actions
walkthrough.text: After the Harvest Phase, the Active Player takes exactly 2 actions. You may choose from four options: Slay a Monster, Recruit a Citizen, Gain a Resource, or Build a Domain. You can take the same action twice if you want. You must take both actions before moving to the End Phase. If any Center Stack runs out as a result of your action, finish the action first, then place an Exhausted card from the Exhausted stack onto the empty position.

walkthrough.title: Action: Slay a Monster
walkthrough.text: Pick a Monster from the top of any stack. Pay Strength tokens equal to the Monster's Strength value (the red shield on the left). You can use Magic tokens to supplement your Strength, but you must spend at least 1 Strength token. Some Monsters also have a Magic cost shown above their Strength. That Magic cost is in addition to any Magic you use to augment Strength. Place the slain Monster in your Victory stack face-up. Then collect the reward shown at the bottom of the card from the Bank or Center Stacks. Some rewards let you take a free Citizen of up to a certain Gold value, ignoring the '+' cost increase.

walkthrough.title: Action: Recruit a Citizen
walkthrough.text: Pick a Citizen from the top of any stack. Pay Gold equal to the base cost printed on the card, plus 1 additional Gold for each copy of that same Citizen you already own. The '+' on the card is a reminder of this increasing cost. You can use Magic to augment Gold, but at least 1 Gold must be spent. Place the Citizen in your tableau. Important: a newly recruited Citizen does not activate on the turn it is recruited. It will start activating from the next turn onward.

walkthrough.title: Action: Gain a Resource
walkthrough.text: This is the simplest action. Take 1 Gold, 1 Strength, or 1 Magic token from the Bank. That is it. This action is useful when you are one resource short of slaying a Monster or recruiting a Citizen, or when nothing else appeals to you.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build a Domain
walkthrough.text: Pick a Domain from the top of any stack. First, check the Role Requirement: your Citizens must have Citizen Role icons (Worker, Soldier, Shadow, Holy) matching those shown on the Domain card. If the Domain shows 2 Soldier icons, you need 2 Citizens with the Soldier role. Starter Peasant and Starter Knight do not have Role icons, so they do not count. Then pay the Gold cost. Magic can augment Gold, but at least 1 Gold must be spent. Place the Domain in your tableau, take any immediate rewards, and flip the next Domain in that stack face-up if one exists.

walkthrough.title: Using Magic as a Wild Resource
walkthrough.text: Magic is the flexible resource in Valeria. You can use Magic tokens in place of Gold (when recruiting Citizens or building Domains) or in place of Strength (when slaying Monsters). The one strict rule is that at least 1 non-Magic token must always be spent. So you cannot slay a Monster using only Magic for the Strength cost. You need at least 1 real Strength token plus any amount of Magic. Same for Gold costs: at least 1 real Gold plus any Magic.

walkthrough.title: End Phase and End-Game Conditions
walkthrough.text: After the Active Player finishes both actions, check whether any end-game condition has been met. The game ends if: all Monsters have been slain, all Domains have been built, or the number of Exhausted stacks equals twice the number of players (for example, 6 Exhausted stacks in a 3-player game). If any condition is met, play continues until all players have taken an equal number of turns, ending with the player to the right of the First Player. Then the Active Player passes the dice to the left and a new turn begins.

walkthrough.title: Scoring
walkthrough.text: When the game is over, each player tallies their score. Add up: the Victory Points printed on all your slain Monsters, the Victory Points on all your built Domains, any Victory Point tokens you collected during the game, and the bonus Victory Points from your secret Duke card. If your Duke scores based on Citizen Role icons, remember to count Role icons on both your Citizens and your Domains. The player with the most Victory Points wins. In the event of a tie, the tied player with the fewest cards in their tableau wins.

summary.title: Turn Overview: Four Phases
bullets: Phase 1: Roll Phase — Active Player rolls 2 dice
Phase 2: Harvest Phase — all players gain resources from Citizens
Phase 3: Action Phase — Active Player takes 2 actions
Phase 4: End Phase — pass dice left, check end-game conditions

summary.title: Roll Phase
bullets: Roll both dice: each die value + the sum activate Citizens
Example: roll 3 and 5 = activate Citizens numbered 3, 5, and 8
Active Player may use Domain powers to change die values (once per Domain per phase)
Announce final dice values to all players

summary.title: Harvest Phase: How Citizens Activate
bullets: All players participate, not just the Active Player
Left power (on-turn): Active Player only
Right power (off-turn): non-Active Players only
Resources taken from/paid to the Bank unless card says otherwise

summary.title: Harvest Phase: Multiple Copies and Doubles
bullets: Multiple copies activate individually (2 Archers = 2 activations)
Doubles: each matching Citizen activates twice (3 Archers + double 4s = 6 activations)
Thief always activates first, before all other Citizens
You may activate Citizens in any order (except Thief first)

summary.title: Harvest Phase: No Activations
bullets: If none of your Citizens activate, take 1 resource of your choice from Bank
Applies to Active and non-Active players alike
You can spend resources mid-Harvest to fuel other Citizen powers

summary.title: Action Phase: Your Two Actions
bullets: Choose 2 from: Slay a Monster, Recruit a Citizen, Gain a Resource, Build a Domain
Same action may be taken twice
Empty stacks get an Exhausted card after the action completes
Must take both actions before End Phase

summary.title: Action: Slay a Monster
bullets: Pay Strength >= Monster's Strength value (Magic can augment, but min 1 Strength)
Some Monsters also cost Magic (in addition to any augmenting)
Collect the reward at bottom of card (Gold, Magic, free Citizens, VP tokens, etc.)
Monster goes face-up in your Victory stack

summary.title: Action: Recruit a Citizen
bullets: Pay base Gold cost + 1 Gold per copy you already own
Magic can augment Gold, but min 1 Gold
Place Citizen in your tableau — does NOT activate the turn it is recruited
The '+' on the card reminds you the price increases with copies

summary.title: Action: Gain a Resource
bullets: Take 1 Gold, 1 Strength, or 1 Magic from the Bank
Useful when 1 token short of a bigger play

summary.title: Action: Build a Domain
bullets: Must have Citizens with matching Role icons (Worker, Soldier, Shadow, Holy)
Starter cards have no Role icons and don't count
Pay Gold cost (Magic can augment, min 1 Gold)
Receive immediate/ongoing power, flip next Domain face-up

summary.title: Using Magic as a Wild Resource
bullets: Magic can substitute for Gold or Strength
At least 1 non-Magic token must always be spent
Cannot pay an entire cost with only Magic tokens

summary.title: End Phase and End-Game Conditions
bullets: Game ends if: all Monsters slain, all Domains built, or Exhausted stacks = 2x player count
Play continues until all players have taken equal turns
Then pass dice left and start a new turn (or score if end triggered)

summary.title: Scoring
bullets: Monster VP + Domain VP + VP tokens + Duke bonus VP
Duke scoring may count Citizen Role icons on Citizens AND Domains
Tiebreaker: fewest cards in tableau wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three example players: Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3. Each player starts with a Starter Peasant (Activation Number 5), a Starter Knight (Activation Number 6), 2 Gold, and 1 Magic. The Center Stacks are set up with the recommended first-game setup. Follow along with the moves and you will have a solid feel for the game.

walkthrough.title: Duke Cards Have Been Dealt
walkthrough.text: Each player has secretly chosen 1 Duke from the 2 they were dealt. For this tutorial, imagine Player 1 has a Duke that scores VP per Soldier Role icon, Player 2 has a Duke that scores VP per slain Monster, and Player 3 has a Duke that scores VP per Domain. These different Dukes will naturally push each player toward different strategies.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Roll Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is the Active Player. They pick up the two dice and roll. The result is a 2 and a 5, which means Activation Numbers 2, 5, and 7 are active this turn. Player 1 does not own any Domains that manipulate dice, so they announce: '2, 5, and 7.'

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Harvest Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is the Active Player, so they use the left-side (on-turn) power of their Citizens. Their Starter Peasant has Activation Number 5 and gives 2 Gold on-turn. Their Starter Knight has Activation Number 6, but 6 was not rolled, so it does not activate. Player 1 gains 2 Gold from the Bank, bringing them to 4 Gold total. Player 1 does not have a Citizen with Activation Number 2 or 7, but since the Peasant activated on the 5, they still got something.

walkthrough.title: Other Players Harvest Too
walkthrough.text: Now Player 2 and Player 3 check their Citizens. They each also have a Starter Peasant (Activation Number 5). Since they are non-Active Players, they use the right-side (off-turn) power, which gives 1 Gold. Both Player 2 and Player 3 gain 1 Gold from the Bank, going from 2 Gold to 3 Gold each. Neither has a Citizen at 2 or 7 either, but that is fine. Everyone got at least something this turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's First Action: Recruit a Citizen
walkthrough.text: Player 1 has 4 Gold and wants to start building their engine. They decide to recruit a Merchant from the Center Stacks. The Merchant has Activation Numbers 2 and 7 (dual activation!) and a base cost of 3 Gold. Since Player 1 does not already own a Merchant, the cost is just 3 Gold. Player 1 pays 3 Gold to the Bank and places the Merchant in their tableau. They now have 1 Gold left. The Merchant will not activate this turn since it was just recruited.

walkthrough.title: Why the Merchant Was a Great Pick
walkthrough.text: The Merchant activates on both 2 and 7. That means whenever any player rolls a combination that includes a 2 or a 7 or sums to 2 or 7, Player 1's Merchant fires. Dual-activation Citizens are incredibly valuable because they trigger more often. Plus, the Merchant is a Worker role, which will help Player 1 meet Domain requirements later.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Action: Gain a Resource
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is low on resources after recruiting. For their second action, they choose Gain a Resource and take 1 Strength from the Bank. This sets them up to potentially slay a Monster on a future turn. Player 1's turn is now complete.

walkthrough.title: End Phase: Pass the Dice
walkthrough.text: No end-game conditions have been met (plenty of Monsters, Domains, and no Exhausted stacks yet), so Player 1 passes the dice to Player 2 on their left. Player 2 is now the Active Player.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Roll Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 2 picks up the dice and rolls a 4 and a 4. Doubles! That means Activation Number 4 fires twice, and the sum is 8, so Activation Number 8 also fires. Player 2 announces: 'Double 4s and an 8.'

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Harvest: Nothing Activates
walkthrough.text: Player 2 checks their tableau: Starter Peasant (5) and Starter Knight (6). Neither 4 nor 8 matches. Since no Citizens activated, Player 2 gets the consolation reward: 1 resource of their choice from the Bank. Player 2 takes 1 Strength. Meanwhile, Player 1 and Player 3 also check. Player 1 has a Peasant (5) and Merchant (2/7) and Knight (6). None match 4 or 8 either, so Player 1 also takes 1 free resource and picks 1 Gold. Player 3 has Peasant (5) and Knight (6). No match. Player 3 takes 1 Magic.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting the Consolation Resource
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake new players make all the time: they forget the consolation resource when none of their Citizens activate. Every time the dice roll does not match any of your Citizens, you get to take 1 Gold, 1 Strength, or 1 Magic for free. It is easy to zone out when it is not your turn, but always check those dice. Free resources add up over the course of a game.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's First Action: Slay a Monster
walkthrough.text: Player 2's Duke scores VP per slain Monster, so they want to start fighting early. The weakest Monster available is the Goblin in the Hills stack with a Strength of 1. Player 2 has 1 Strength token and 2 Magic. They pay 1 Strength to slay the Goblin. The reward at the bottom of the Goblin card gives Player 2 1 Gold from the Bank. The Goblin goes into Player 2's Victory stack.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Second Action: Gain a Resource
walkthrough.text: Player 2 takes 1 Strength from the Bank to rebuild for future Monster slaying. Player 2's turn is over. The dice pass left to Player 3.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Roll Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 3 rolls a 1 and a 5, activating Citizens numbered 1, 5, and 6. Player 3 announces: '1, 5, and 6.'

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Harvest Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 3 is the Active Player. Their Starter Peasant (5) gives 2 Gold on-turn. Their Starter Knight (6) gives 1 Strength on-turn. Nice, two Citizens fired! Player 3 collects 2 Gold and 1 Strength from the Bank. Players 1 and 2 check their off-turn powers. Player 1's Peasant (5) gives 1 Gold off-turn. Player 1's Merchant (2/7) does not match. Player 1's Knight (6) gives 1 Strength off-turn. Player 1 gets 1 Gold and 1 Strength. Player 2's Peasant (5) gives 1 Gold and Knight (6) gives 1 Strength off-turn. Player 2 also collects.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Off-Turn Harvesting
walkthrough.text: Another common mistake: non-Active Players forget to harvest on other people's turns. You should always be paying attention to the dice results, even when it is not your turn. Every time dice are rolled, check your Citizens. The off-turn powers are slightly weaker than on-turn powers, but they fire on every single player's turn, which means you activate them far more often over the course of a game.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's First Action: Build a Domain
walkthrough.text: Player 3's Duke scores for Domains, so they want to build one early. They look at the face-up Domains in the bottom row and find one that requires 1 Worker icon and costs 4 Gold. Player 3 checks their tableau. They have a Starter Peasant and Starter Knight, but remember: Starter cards have no Role icons! Player 3 cannot build any Domain yet because they have no Citizens with Role icons.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Starter Cards Have No Roles
walkthrough.text: This trips people up regularly. The Starter Peasant and Starter Knight look similar to regular Citizens, but they do not have Role icons in the upper right corner. You cannot count them toward Domain requirements. To build Domains, you need to recruit regular Citizens first. Player 3 realizes this and changes their plan.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Revised First Action: Recruit a Citizen
walkthrough.text: Player 3 pivots and recruits a Cleric instead. The Cleric has Activation Number 1, costs 2 Gold base, and has the Holy Role icon. Player 3 pays 2 Gold and places the Cleric in their tableau. Now they have a Holy icon, which will help meet Domain requirements on future turns.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Action: Recruit Another Citizen
walkthrough.text: Player 3 still has resources and decides to recruit again. They pick up a Peasant from the Center Stacks. The base cost is 2 Gold, but Player 3 already has a Starter Peasant. Wait, does the Starter Peasant count? Yes! The '+' cost increase counts all cards with the same name, including Starters. So the cost is 2 + 1 = 3 Gold. Player 3 pays 1 Gold and 2 Magic (remember, Magic can augment Gold but at least 1 Gold must be spent). The Peasant has a Worker Role icon, so Player 3 now has both Holy and Worker roles.

walkthrough.title: End of Round 1 Check
walkthrough.text: After Player 3's turn, the dice would pass back to Player 1 for the next round. Let's check the game state. Player 1 has a Merchant, Starter Peasant, and Starter Knight. Player 2 has slain a Goblin and is building Strength. Player 3 has recruited a Cleric and a Peasant and is setting up for Domains. Each player is pursuing a strategy aligned with their secret Duke. This is exactly how Valeria should feel in the early rounds.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways from Round 1
walkthrough.text: Here is what we learned. First, the Harvest Phase involves all players every turn, so stay engaged. Second, dual-activation Citizens like the Merchant fire more often and are excellent early picks. Third, Magic is incredibly flexible and lets you stretch your budget. Fourth, check your Duke early and let it guide your strategy. And fifth, Starter cards do not have Role icons, so you need regular Citizens before you can build Domains.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen a complete round of Valeria: Card Kingdoms. You practiced rolling dice, harvesting resources on-turn and off-turn, recruiting Citizens, slaying a Monster, and learning why you cannot rush into Domains without Role icons. You also caught three common mistakes that trip up new players. You are ready to keep playing on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You March Into Battle
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and resource management advice.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every card type, keyword, and icon in the game.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun conquering Valeria!

bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players demonstrating different strategies
Each starts with Starter Peasant, Starter Knight, 2 Gold, 1 Magic
Recommended first-game Center Stacks setup

summary.title: Duke Cards Have Been Dealt
bullets: Player 1: Duke scores VP per Soldier Role icon
Player 2: Duke scores VP per slain Monster
Player 3: Duke scores VP per Domain
Different Dukes drive different strategies

summary.title: Player 1's Roll Phase
bullets: Rolls 2 and 5 — activates Citizens numbered 2, 5, and 7
No Domain powers to use yet
Announces dice results to all players

summary.title: Player 1's Harvest Phase
bullets: Starter Peasant (5) fires on-turn: gain 2 Gold
Starter Knight (6) does not match — no activation
Player 1 now has 4 Gold total

summary.title: Other Players Harvest Too
bullets: Players 2 and 3 use off-turn power on Starter Peasant (5): gain 1 Gold each
No other Citizens match 2, 5, or 7

summary.title: Player 1's First Action: Recruit a Citizen
bullets: Recruits Merchant (Activation 2/7, base cost 3 Gold)
Pays 3 Gold, places Merchant in tableau
Merchant does not activate this turn (recruited Citizens skip their first turn)

summary.title: Why the Merchant Was a Great Pick
bullets: Dual-activation (2 and 7) means more frequent triggers
Worker Role icon helps meet Domain requirements
More activations = more resources over the game

summary.title: Player 1's Second Action: Gain a Resource
bullets: Takes 1 Strength from the Bank
Setting up for future Monster slaying
Simple action but keeps the strategy moving

summary.title: End Phase: Pass the Dice
bullets: No end-game conditions met
Dice pass left to Player 2
New turn begins

summary.title: Player 2's Roll Phase
bullets: Rolls double 4s — Activation Number 4 fires twice, sum 8 also fires
Doubles are powerful: matching Citizens activate twice

summary.title: Player 2's Harvest: Nothing Activates
bullets: No Citizens match 4 or 8 for any player
All players take 1 consolation resource of their choice
Player 2: 1 Strength, Player 1: 1 Gold, Player 3: 1 Magic

summary.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting the Consolation Resource
bullets: If no Citizens activate, take 1 free resource (Gold, Strength, or Magic)
Applies to ALL players, Active and non-Active
Easy to miss — always check the dice even off-turn

summary.title: Player 2's First Action: Slay a Monster
bullets: Slays the Goblin (Strength 1) by paying 1 Strength
Collects reward: 1 Gold from the Bank
Goblin goes into Victory stack — scores VP at end of game

summary.title: Player 2's Second Action: Gain a Resource
bullets: Takes 1 Strength from the Bank
Rebuilding for future Monster fights
Dice pass to Player 3

summary.title: Player 3's Roll Phase
bullets: Rolls 1 and 5 — activates Citizens numbered 1, 5, and 6
Great roll: hits both Starter cards for all players

summary.title: Player 3's Harvest Phase
bullets: Player 3 (Active): Peasant (5) = 2 Gold, Knight (6) = 1 Strength
Players 1 and 2 (off-turn): Peasant (5) = 1 Gold, Knight (6) = 1 Strength
Everyone benefits when 5 and 6 are rolled

summary.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Off-Turn Harvesting
bullets: Non-Active Players must check dice results every turn
Off-turn powers are weaker but fire on every player's turn

summary.title: Player 3's First Action: Build a Domain
bullets: Tries to build a Domain but cannot — no Role icons in tableau
Starter Peasant and Starter Knight have no Role icons
Must recruit regular Citizens first to gain Role icons

summary.title: Common Mistake: Starter Cards Have No Roles
bullets: Starter cards look like Citizens but have no Role icons
Cannot use them to meet Domain Role requirements
Must recruit regular Citizens with Worker/Soldier/Shadow/Holy icons

summary.title: Player 3's Revised First Action: Recruit a Citizen
bullets: Recruits Cleric (Activation 1, base cost 2 Gold, Holy Role)
Now has a Holy icon for Domain requirements
Adapts plan after learning about Role icons

summary.title: Player 3's Second Action: Recruit Another Citizen
bullets: Recruits Peasant for 3 Gold (base 2 + 1 for existing Starter Peasant)
Pays 1 Gold + 2 Magic (Magic augments Gold, min 1 Gold)
Now has Worker Role + Holy Role — Domain-ready

summary.title: End of Round 1 Check
bullets: Player 1: Merchant acquired, engine building with dual-activation
Player 2: Goblin slain, Monster-hunting strategy underway
Player 3: Cleric + Peasant recruited, setting up for Domains

summary.title: Key Takeaways from Round 1
bullets: Harvest Phase involves ALL players every turn — stay engaged
Dual-activation Citizens fire more often and are great early picks
Magic is flexible — stretches your budget across Gold and Strength
Check your Duke early and let it guide your strategy
Starter cards have no Role icons — recruit Citizens before building Domains

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have practiced rolling, harvesting, recruiting, slaying, and Domain-checking
Three common mistakes covered: consolation resource, off-turn harvesting, Starter roles
You are ready to play a full game on your own

summary.title: Before You March Into Battle
bullets: Check out General Tips for strategy basics
Reference the Appendix for keywords, icons, and card details
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers

walkthrough.title: Read Your Duke First
walkthrough.text: Before you make any decisions, read your Duke card carefully. Your Duke is your secret scoring multiplier and can swing 10 to 20 points at the end of the game. If your Duke scores for Soldier Role icons, you should prioritize recruiting Soldiers and building Domains that feature Soldier icons. If your Duke scores per Monster slain, focus on fighting early and often. Every action you take should be filtered through the question: does this help my Duke score? Players who ignore their Duke consistently lose to players who build around it.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Dual-Activation Citizens
walkthrough.text: Some Citizens have two Activation Numbers instead of one, like the Merchant (2/7) or the Rogue (7/12). These Citizens are gold mines because they trigger on more dice combinations than single-activation Citizens. Over the course of a game with many dice rolls, a dual-activation Citizen might fire twice as often as a single-activation one. When choosing between two Citizens of similar cost, almost always pick the dual-activation option. The extra harvests compound into a significant resource advantage.

walkthrough.title: Stay Engaged on Other Players' Turns
walkthrough.text: One of the things that makes Valeria special is that you harvest resources on every player's turn, not just your own. In a 4-player game, you harvest four times per round but only take actions once. That means your off-turn income often exceeds your on-turn income. Pay attention to every dice roll. Make sure you are collecting your off-turn power from every activated Citizen. And do not forget the consolation resource when nothing activates. Zoning out during other turns is the number one way new players leave resources on the table.

walkthrough.title: Use Magic Wisely
walkthrough.text: Magic is the most flexible resource in Valeria because it can substitute for Gold or Strength. But here is the catch: you must always spend at least 1 non-Magic token. So Magic works best as a supplement, not a replacement. Hoard Magic when you can and deploy it for big purchases where you are 1 or 2 short. Magic is particularly powerful for building expensive Domains, where the Gold cost can be 8 to 12. A stockpile of 3 or 4 Magic makes those big plays possible without needing a massive Gold reserve.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Exhausted Stack Count
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the number of Exhausted stacks equals twice the player count. In a 3-player game, that is 6 Exhausted stacks. This can sneak up on you. Keep an eye on how many stacks are getting depleted. If the game is about to end and you have resources sitting unused, spend them fast. Conversely, if you have a lead and see the Exhausted count approaching the threshold, you can strategically help deplete stacks to trigger the end before opponents catch up.

walkthrough.title: Balance Recruiting and Slaying
walkthrough.text: New players tend to either recruit too many Citizens or slay too many Monsters, but rarely do both well. Citizens generate resources that fuel your engine, while Monsters provide Victory Points and immediate rewards. The best players find a balance. A good rule of thumb: in the first few rounds, focus on recruiting 2 to 3 Citizens to build your resource engine. Then start slaying Monsters once you have steady Strength income. Domains should come in the mid-to-late game once you have the Role icons and Gold to support them.

walkthrough.title: Don't Neglect Strength
walkthrough.text: It is tempting to recruit Citizens constantly because they power up your Harvest Phase. But remember that slaying Monsters is the primary source of Victory Points, and you need Strength to do it. If you spend every action recruiting Citizens and never accumulate Strength, you will fall behind on scoring. Make sure at least some of your Citizens generate Strength on activation, or plan to spend actions taking Strength from the Bank. The mid-game transition from building your engine to cashing in for Monsters is critical.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Domain Requirements Ahead
walkthrough.text: Domains are expensive but powerful. They provide Victory Points, and some have game-changing ongoing powers. Before you try to build a Domain, scan the available ones and note what Role icons they need. Then recruit Citizens that happen to fill those Role requirements. This way, your Citizens serve double duty: generating resources during Harvest and qualifying you for Domains. If your Duke also scores for Role icons, this triple alignment of Citizens, Domains, and Duke scoring is the sweet spot for big scores.

summary.title: Read Your Duke First
bullets: Duke can swing 10-20 VP at game end
Filter every decision through: does this help my Duke?
If Duke scores per Soldier, recruit Soldiers; if per Monster, slay early
Players who ignore their Duke lose to players who build around it

summary.title: Prioritize Dual-Activation Citizens
bullets: Dual-activation Citizens (e.g., Merchant 2/7, Rogue 7/12) trigger on more rolls
Over many dice rolls, they fire roughly twice as often
When cost is similar, almost always pick dual-activation
Extra harvests compound into major resource advantage

summary.title: Stay Engaged on Other Players' Turns
bullets: You harvest on EVERY player's turn, not just yours
In a 4-player game, 4 harvests per round but only 1 action phase
Off-turn income often exceeds on-turn income over the game
Don't forget consolation resource when nothing activates

summary.title: Use Magic Wisely
bullets: Magic substitutes for Gold or Strength, but min 1 non-Magic always required
Best used as supplement for big purchases (expensive Domains, tough Monsters)
Hoard Magic and deploy for 1-2 token gaps
Particularly powerful for Domain costs of 8-12 Gold

summary.title: Watch the Exhausted Stack Count
bullets: Game ends when Exhausted stacks = 2x player count (e.g., 6 in a 3-player game)
Can sneak up — track depleted stacks constantly
If behind: slow the game by avoiding stack depletion
If ahead: strategically help exhaust stacks to end the game

summary.title: Balance Recruiting and Slaying
bullets: Citizens = resource engine; Monsters = Victory Points
Early game: recruit 2-3 Citizens to build your engine
Mid game: transition to slaying Monsters for VP
Late game: build Domains once you have Role icons and Gold

summary.title: Don't Neglect Strength
bullets: Slaying Monsters is the primary VP source and requires Strength
Ensure some Citizens generate Strength on activation
Mid-game transition from engine-building to Monster-slaying is critical
Falling behind on Strength means falling behind on scoring

summary.title: Plan Your Domain Requirements Ahead
bullets: Scan available Domains and note their Role icon requirements
Recruit Citizens that fill both resource-generation and Role-requirement needs
Triple alignment: Citizens + Domains + Duke scoring = big points
Domains have powerful ongoing abilities that compound over remaining turns

walkthrough.title: Activation Number Coverage
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think about probability coverage. With two dice, the most common sums are 6, 7, and 8. The least common are 2 and 12. Each individual die shows 1 through 6 equally. So when you recruit Citizens, consider how many of the 11 possible activation numbers (2 through 12) your tableau covers. Gaps in coverage mean missed harvests. Dual-activation Citizens are premium because they cover two numbers. A tableau with Citizens at 2/7, 5, 6, 8, and 9/10 covers seven numbers and will rarely have a completely dead turn.

walkthrough.title: The Stacking Strategy
walkthrough.text: Instead of spreading your Citizens across many activation numbers, some players intentionally stack multiple copies of the same Citizen. Why? Because when doubles are rolled, each copy fires twice. Three copies of a Citizen on a doubles roll means six activations. This strategy is expensive because recruiting cost increases by 1 Gold per copy, but it creates massive burst turns. It pairs well with Citizens that have strong on-turn powers, like gaining 3 Gold per activation. The risk is that when your number does not hit, you get nothing.

walkthrough.title: Monster Timing and Reward Chaining
walkthrough.text: Do not just slay Monsters for Victory Points. Pay close attention to the rewards. Some Monsters give you a free Citizen of up to a certain Gold value, which saves you an entire action. Others give Victory Point tokens based on how many Monsters you have slain from the same Area. If you plan your Monster kills so that you slay the Boss Monster last (it is on the bottom of the stack and typically has an Area-count reward), you maximize those bonus VPs. Timing your Monster kills around reward synergies is what separates good players from great ones.

walkthrough.title: Domain Power Combos
walkthrough.text: Some Domain cards have ongoing powers that change the rules of the game in your favor. The Palace of the Dawn lets you reduce a die value by 1. Foxgrove Palisade lets you set a die to 6 for 2 Gold. The Desert Orchid lets you set a die to 1 for 0 Gold if you have no Holy Citizens. These die-manipulation Domains are incredibly powerful because they let you target specific activation numbers every turn. Combining multiple die-manipulation Domains gives you near-complete control over the Harvest Phase. Build these Domains early and they pay for themselves in resource efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: In Valeria, information is mostly public. You can see what Citizens everyone has recruited, which Monsters they have slain, and which Domains they have built. Use this information. If an opponent is stacking Soldiers, they are probably racing for a specific Domain or their Duke scores on Soldiers. If someone is hoarding Strength, they are about to slay an expensive Monster. Knowing what your opponents are planning lets you anticipate which stacks will run out and whether to accelerate or slow down the game.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the End Game
walkthrough.text: The three end conditions are: all Monsters slain, all Domains built, or Exhausted stacks equal to twice the player count. Advanced players manipulate which condition triggers and when. If you are ahead, push to end the game quickly by depleting stacks. If you are behind, recruit Citizens from full stacks to avoid creating Exhausted piles, and slay Monsters from stacks that still have multiple cards. Some Domain cards like Watcher on the Water or Nest of the Weaver Woman let you return cards to the Center Stacks, which can extend the game by removing Exhausted cards.

walkthrough.title: Duke-Specific Builds
walkthrough.text: Once you know your Duke, build your entire strategy around it. A Duke that scores for Shadow Role icons means you should recruit Shadow Citizens (Thief, Rogue, etc.) and build Domains with Shadow icons. A Duke that scores per Monster Type (Boss or Titan) means you should target specific Monsters. A Duke that scores per Domain means Domains are your priority even though they are expensive. The best players mentally calculate their Duke's projected score every few turns and adjust their remaining actions to maximize it. Your Duke is worth more than any single Monster or Domain.

walkthrough.title: The Magic Economy at High Levels
walkthrough.text: At higher levels of play, Magic management becomes a critical differentiator. Because Magic supplements both Gold and Strength, a player with 5 Magic tokens has enormous flexibility. They can pivot from buying a 6-Gold Domain to slaying a 5-Strength Monster depending on what the board state demands. Citizens that generate Magic (like the Monk, who converts Gold to Magic, or the Wizard) are underrated by new players. Building a Magic-heavy economy gives you options that a Gold-heavy or Strength-heavy player simply does not have. Flexibility wins games.

summary.title: Activation Number Coverage
bullets: Most common sums: 6, 7, 8. Least common: 2, 12
Cover as many of the 11 activation numbers (2-12) as possible
Dual-activation Citizens are premium for coverage
Gaps = missed harvests = wasted turns

summary.title: The Stacking Strategy
bullets: Stack multiple copies of the same Citizen for burst turns
Doubles roll + 3 copies = 6 activations in one Harvest
Cost increases by +1 Gold per copy owned
High risk, high reward — dead turns when number doesn't hit

summary.title: Monster Timing and Reward Chaining
bullets: Monster rewards include free Citizens, Gold, Magic, and VP tokens
Boss Monsters (bottom of stack) reward VP per same-Area Monsters slain
Slay the Boss last to maximize Area-count bonus VP
Free Citizen rewards save you an entire action

summary.title: Domain Power Combos
bullets: Die-manipulation Domains (Palace of the Dawn, Foxgrove Palisade, Desert Orchid)
Let you target specific activation numbers every turn
Combine multiple for near-complete Harvest Phase control
Build early — they pay for themselves in resource efficiency

summary.title: Reading Your Opponents
bullets: Citizens, slain Monsters, and Domains are all public information
Stacking Soldiers = Domain race or Soldier-scoring Duke
Hoarding Strength = about to slay an expensive Monster
Anticipate stack depletion to plan your actions

bullets: 3 end conditions: all Monsters slain, all Domains built, Exhausted = 2x players
If ahead: deplete stacks to end game quickly
If behind: take from full stacks to avoid creating Exhausted piles
Domains like Watcher on the Water can extend the game by returning cards

summary.title: Duke-Specific Builds

Shadow Duke = recruit Shadow Citizens + Shadow Domains
Monster Duke = slay early and often, target specific Monster Types
Calculate projected Duke score every few turns and adjust

summary.title: The Magic Economy at High Levels
bullets: Magic supplements both Gold and Strength = maximum flexibility
5 Magic tokens lets you pivot between Domain-buying and Monster-slaying
Magic-generating Citizens (Monk, Wizard) are underrated by new players
Flexibility wins games — Magic-heavy players have more options per turn

entries.definition: The player whose turn it currently is. The Active Player rolls the dice, uses on-turn Citizen powers during Harvest, takes 2 actions, and then passes the dice to the left. The Active Player may also use Domain powers to manipulate dice during the Roll Phase.
Any player who is not the Active Player during the current turn. Non-Active Players use the right-side (off-turn) Citizen powers during the Harvest Phase. They do not take actions.

The number(s) in the upper left corner of a Citizen card. When a die value or the sum of both dice matches this number, the Citizen activates during the Harvest Phase. Some Citizens have two Activation Numbers.

The 4-row by 5-column grid of card stacks at the center of the table. Top row: Monsters. Middle two rows: Citizens. Bottom row: Domains. Players take cards from the top of these stacks during actions.
A player's personal collection of cards in front of them, including Starter cards, recruited Citizens, built Domains, and their secret Duke. The tableau represents your kingdom.
The face-up pile of slain Monster cards next to a player's tableau. Monsters in the Victory stack are public information and are scored at the end of the game.
The shared pool of Gold, Strength, Magic, and Victory Point tokens. Resources are taken from and paid to the Bank unless a card specifies otherwise. Resources and VP tokens are considered unlimited.

A placeholder card placed on an empty Center Stack position when a stack runs out during the game. The number of Exhausted stacks is one of the three end-game triggers. Placed from the Exhausted stack above the Monster row.
Tokens (x5, etc.) used when resource tokens run out. Place a resource token on a Multiplier to represent that many of that resource. Resources and VP are unlimited in supply.
A token given to the randomly selected starting player. Determines turn order (clockwise from this player). Also marks the player who takes the final turn when end-game conditions are triggered.
One of the three resources. Used to recruit Citizens and build Domains. Represented by gold-colored tokens. Can be augmented by Magic. Most Citizens generate Gold through their harvest powers.
One of the three resources. Used to slay Monsters. Represented by red tokens. Can be augmented by Magic, but at least 1 Strength must be spent when slaying a Monster.

One of the three resources. The flexible wild resource that can substitute for Gold or Strength. At least 1 non-Magic token must always be spent. Some Monsters have an additional Magic cost on top of their Strength cost.
The currency of victory. Earned from slain Monsters, built Domains, VP tokens, and Duke end-game scoring. Some card rewards grant VP tokens immediately. The player with the most VP wins.
Phase 1 of each turn. The Active Player rolls both dice. Each die value and the sum activate Citizens. The Active Player may use Domain powers to change dice before announcing final results.

Phase 2 of each turn. All players gain resources from activated Citizens. Active Player uses on-turn (left) powers; non-Active Players use off-turn (right) powers. If no Citizens activate, take 1 free resource.
Phase 3 of each turn. The Active Player takes exactly 2 actions: Slay a Monster, Recruit a Citizen, Gain a Resource, or Build a Domain. Same action may be taken twice. Both must be completed.
Phase 4 of each turn. Check end-game conditions. If met, play continues until all players have taken equal turns. The Active Player passes the dice to the left.

An action. Pay Strength (and sometimes Magic) equal to the Monster's cost. Place the Monster in your Victory stack. Collect the reward at the bottom of the card. Magic can augment Strength but at least 1 Strength must be spent.

An action. Pay Gold equal to the base cost plus 1 Gold per copy you already own. Place the Citizen in your tableau. The Citizen does not activate on the turn it is recruited. Magic can augment Gold but at least 1 Gold must be spent.
An action. Take 1 Gold, 1 Strength, or 1 Magic token from the Bank. The simplest action, useful when you are 1 resource short of a bigger play.

An action. Must meet the Role icon requirement with Citizens in your tableau (Starter cards do not count). Pay the Gold cost. Place Domain in tableau. Take immediate rewards if any. Flip the next Domain face-up. Domain powers may be ongoing or one-time.

The left-side power at the bottom of a Citizen card, under the sword icon. Only the Active Player uses this power when the Citizen activates during the Harvest Phase. Generally stronger than off-turn powers.

The right-side power at the bottom of a Citizen card, under the shield icon. Only non-Active Players use this power when the Citizen activates. Weaker individually but fires on every other player's turn.
A one-time benefit received when you slay a Monster or build a Domain. Shown at the bottom of the card. May include resources, free Citizens, VP tokens, or additional actions.
A persistent benefit on a Domain card that activates repeatedly as stated on the card. These may trigger during specific phases or under certain conditions for the rest of the game.

One of the 108 cards representing people of Valeria. Have an Activation Number, a Role icon, a Gold cost, and two powers (on-turn and off-turn). Sorted by name in the Center Stacks. 5 cards per stack in a standard game.

One of the 48 cards representing enemies threatening Valeria. Have an Area icon, a Strength cost (some also Magic), a reward, and Victory Points. Sorted by Area and arranged weakest on top, strongest on bottom.

One of the 24 cards representing buildings and territories. Have Role icon requirements, a Gold cost, a power (immediate or ongoing), and Victory Points. Shuffled and dealt face-down with 1 face-up per stack.

One of the 10 cards representing the player's secret identity. Kept hidden until end-game scoring. Each Duke has a unique condition that awards bonus Victory Points based on icons, card types, or other criteria.

The initial Starter Peasant and Starter Knight given to each player during setup. They have Activation Numbers and powers but no Role icons. You always have exactly 1 of each. They cannot be lost or gained.
A player aid card summarizing the turn phases, actions, and key rules. One per player. Not part of gameplay.
One of the four Citizen Role icons. Found on Citizens like Peasant, Merchant, Blacksmith, and Miner. Required by some Domain cards. May be scored by certain Duke cards.
One of the four Citizen Role icons. Found on Citizens like Knight, Mercenary, Archer, Champion, and Warlord. Required by some Domain cards. May be scored by certain Duke cards.
One of the four Citizen Role icons. Found on Citizens like Rogue, Thief, and Butcher. Required by some Domain cards. May be scored by certain Duke cards.
One of the four Citizen Role icons. Found on Citizens like Cleric, Monk, Paladin, and Priestess. Required by some Domain cards. May be scored by certain Duke cards.
The location icon in the upper right of each Monster card. Eight areas in the base game: Hills, Ruins, Forest, Swamp, Mountains, Caverns, Barrens, Valley. Monsters are sorted into stacks by Area.
An icon below the Area icon on Monster cards. Four types in the base game: Boss, Minion, Beast, and Titan. Boss and Titan are referenced on some Duke cards for end-game scoring.
A Monster Type found on the strongest Monster in each Area stack (bottom of the stack). Boss Monsters often give rewards based on how many Monsters you have slain from the same Area.
A Monster Type. The most common type, representing standard creatures. No special rules apply to Minions in the base game.
A Monster Type. Represents animal-like creatures. No special rules in the base game, but may be referenced by Duke cards.
A Monster Type. Represents the most powerful creatures. Referenced on some Duke cards for end-game scoring.
When both dice show the same value. Doubles activate each matching Citizen twice, resulting in double the normal harvest from those Citizens. Very valuable for stacking strategies.
The total of both dice added together. Along with each individual die value, the sum activates matching Citizens. For example, rolling 3 and 5 activates Citizens numbered 3, 5, and 8.

Some Domain cards allow the Active Player to change die values during the Roll Phase. Each die-changing Domain may only be used once per Roll Phase. Examples: Palace of the Dawn (reduce by 1), Foxgrove Palisade (set to 6 for 2 Gold).

The '+' on Citizen Gold costs reminds players that the cost increases by 1 Gold for each copy of the same Citizen already in their tableau. When a Monster reward grants a free Citizen 'up to' a value, ignore the '+' and use the base cost only.
An operator icon meaning both items on either side are included. Read as 'plus.' For example, a reward showing Gold AND Magic means you receive both.
An operator icon meaning you choose one of the listed items. Read as 'or.' For example, a reward showing Gold OR Magic means you pick one.
An operator icon meaning the left item is multiplied by the right. Read as 'per.' For example, 1 VP per Monster means you get 1 VP for each Monster you have slain.
An operator icon meaning take the item on the left with the limit set by the right. When taking a Citizen with this icon, ignore the '+' cost and go by base cost only.
An operator icon meaning the item on the left is paid to the Bank to gain the item on the right. Read as 'paid to gain.'
An operator icon meaning the item on the left is taken from the item on the right. Read as 'taken from.' Used primarily by the Thief.
An operator icon meaning the Active Player chooses an opponent for the effect to target. Read as 'an opponent of your choice.'
An operator icon meaning the Active Player chooses a die for the effect to target. Read as 'any die.'
If a player's dice roll does not activate any of their Citizens, that player takes 1 resource of their choice (Gold, Strength, or Magic) from the Bank. Applies to both Active and non-Active Players.
If there is a conflict between the rulebook and the text on a Domain card, the Domain card's text takes priority. Always follow what the Domain says.
When an end-game condition is met, play continues until all players have had an equal number of turns. The game ends after the player to the right of the First Player finishes their turn.
In the event of a tied final score, the player with the fewest cards in their tableau wins. This includes all Starter, Citizen, Domain, and Duke cards.

A Citizen card with a unique power: when activated, the Thief takes up to 3 Gold or 3 Magic (not mixed) from an opponent of your choice. The Thief always activates first, before any other Citizens during the Harvest Phase.
A Citizen card that converts Gold to Magic. The on-turn power typically lets you pay Gold to gain Magic at a favorable rate. Holy Role icon. Activation Number 1.
A Domain card with an ongoing power that lets you reduce the value of one die by 1 during the Roll Phase. You may not reduce a die to zero. Can be combined with other die-manipulation Domains.
A Domain card that lets you change one die to a 6 by paying 2 Gold during the Roll Phase.
A Domain card that lets you change one die to a 1 by paying 1 Gold per Holy Citizen you own. If you own no Holy Citizens, the cost is 0 Gold.

A Domain card that gives you a Victory Point whenever any player rolls doubles or when dice are changed to be doubles. Can trigger more than once if doubles are changed to a different pair of doubles.
A Domain card that lets you take a random Monster from any one player's Victory stack. You do not receive the Monster's reward. The stolen Monster goes into your Victory stack.

A Domain card that lets you return a Monster from your Victory stack to its original Area in the Center Stacks. If that stack was Exhausted, remove the Exhausted card first. Can extend the game by un-exhausting a stack.
A Domain card that lets you return a Citizen from your Victory stack to its original area in the Center Stacks. If the stack was Exhausted, remove the Exhausted card. Can extend the game.
A Monster Area. In the recommended first-game setup: 3 Goblin, 1 Goblin Mage, 1 Goblin King (arranged weakest on top).
A Monster Area. In the recommended first-game setup: 2 Skeleton, 2 Flaming Skeleton, 1 Skeleton King.
A Monster Area. In the recommended first-game setup: 2 Treant, 2 Bane Spider, 1 Spider Queen.
A Monster Area. In the recommended first-game setup: 3 Owlbear, 1 Giant, 1 Troll.
A Monster Area. In the recommended first-game setup: 2 Dire Bear, 2 Orc Warrior, 1 Orc Chieftain.
A Monster Area. Contains creatures like the Gelatinous Cube. Not in the recommended first-game setup.
A Monster Area. Not in the recommended first-game setup. Available for variety in future games.
A Monster Area. Not in the recommended first-game setup. Features in the Mastery Over Magic and Monster Blood recommended setups.
